item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 because I can imagine myself crying while chopping shallot with sad music in the background.

item.title: Song Spoilers
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

It seems like the consensus is the shallot/onion is singing the song, but I was thinking it was a sapient farmhouse that like, somehow followed this onion's journey (spiritually?) from the plowman's fields and plays this song for the next people to interact with it? For instance, I swore there was a line in the song that described how the farmhouse witnessed the plowman plow straight through an anthill and killed the queen. I could be making that up but it was so dramatic in my head… my goodness the song is unexpectedly awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Have to admit, I wasn't incredibly enamored with this one most of the way through, as I thought it was a bit light and silly without a ton of depth, but the "example recording" was absolutely brilliant. My attitude did a 180 on the entire piece after about thirty seconds. It pulls the entire article together into something truly excellent, in the most unexpected way possible. Hard +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Belated, true. Though, given how the song shows everything has sapience, I wonder if the last resort is going breathairean.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i just got feels for a song about an onion in a field getting harvested… +1 this was beautiful

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: Making it a reproduction by affected personel would be more believeable imo

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Shallots that make one cry, not due to chemical irritants, but on account of the music they produce, is pretty amusing, sensible in terms of functionality and form, and cute as hell. I don't have the opportunity to check out the song itself at the moment, but reading the transcription conveys its purpose effectively.

My downvote primarily comes in due to the interview, which serves no overall narrative purpose besides covering the ground of how it was discovered. There's not necessarilly anything wrong with the dialogue itself (besides the repeated assurance that our interviewee enjoys metal - once, I feel, was enough), but what it does do is inject a ton of fluff that doesn't hold up to the rest of the work, weakening the impact of the final addendum.

I believe you'd have something more compact and efficient if that were to be excised completely, given that it doesn't contribute to the pathos imparted in the shallot's song.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some explicit influences in this song are Jump Little Children and Nickel Creek.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Pros
- Hide

- The formatting for the addenda looks awesome.
- The song's chorus felt great to listen to.
- The bridge sounds much better than the chorus.
- The lyrics that overlap the chorus after the bridge is like, this harmonious payoff from the bridge's buildup for this whole song.

The Cons
- Hide

- When I first heard the song, it felt like I was listening to something straight out of an Alvin and The Chipmunks movie, but it does grow on you after a few listens.
- The verses sounded rushed and had rather awkward pacing for it. I didn't really care too much for it.
- More of a nitpick but, there are parts of lyrics that don't fit with the actual song's lyrics like how the singer delivered it(Could be you, or someone else, I dunno) .


All in all, the song really hit it well. Probably one of the best ways music could be incorporated an SCP. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wouldn't the knowledge that a shallot could sing make you less likely to go vegan—or at least make you eat less plants from same genus?

Like, one of the argument people make to convince others to go vegan is that "is it ethical to subject animals to pain for food?" but here you're confronted with the fact that this plant not only feel pain of loss, it also have advanced mental faculty sufficient to write a song, effectively making eating them transgression against sapient entities and blatantly unethical.

Me, I like my fried shallots.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Quit tryin' to convince me to go vegan!

+1. That vivid imagery and build-up is fantastic. The great damn song helps, too. I know what I'll say next , but I'm getting Kansas vibes and some other band I can't quite put my name on. Who're your inspirations, if you don't mind me asking?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A pretty simple anomaly, but an arresting one nonetheless. +1

This shallot will make you cry when you cut it, but not for the reason you might expect.

Superb work, the part on how it's discovered is a bit weak imo, but not enough to detract much from the entire piece. The music as well is really good, and the lyric is strong, enough to fulfill the expectation from the main description—I expect music that could make me cry, and you deliver.

I wish you'd include how exactly the music was recorded tho, as the first paragraph of description explicitly said that the audio cannot be detected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Credits
- Hide

Image: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Shallots_-_sliced_and_whole.jpg, unedited

Music is original. Included loops are from Looperman, are Creative Commons, and are used with permission:

  • MINOR2GO - Jephana
  • MINOR2GO - Book of the Lost Fairytales
  • MINOR2GO - Poisoned Memory

Thanks to those who looked it over.
